prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One idea that is often overrated in modern knowledge work is the notion of working longer hours in pursuit of greater productivity and output. The standard wisdom holds that if you can work on a task for a full eight hours every day, you are automatically a time management and productivity champion.

This idea is overrated because the reality is that the human brain's capacity for focused, high-quality work is limited. Studies have consistently shown that the most productive and creative workers are not those who work in long stretches but those who work in concentrated periods of time, followed by periods of rest and recovery.

The idea that you can work for eight hours of focused time is akin to believing that you can run at high speed for a full hour without needing to slow down or take a break. It is simply not feasible for most people to maintain high levels of concentration and creativity for such extended periods.

Furthermore, the quality of one’s work is not only influenced by the quantity of time spent on a task, but also by the quality of that time. Working on a task for hours on end, without any breaks, often leads to a decrease in both concentration levels and creativity. This is because the human brain is not capable of maintaining the same level of focus and creativity for extended periods. In other words, more hours worked does not always equate to better quality output.

In summary, while working longer hours may seem like a good idea in terms of demonstrating commitment to one's work, it is often an overrated practice in modern knowledge work. Instead, the most productive and creative workers are those who work in concentrated periods of time, followed by periods of rest and recovery. This approach allows them to maintain high levels of concentration and creativity throughout the day, leading to higher quality output.
